refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall enable execution of the load→travel→dump cycle within the customer-defined cycle time target.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) supporting/enabling the capability to achieve cycle time, and (2) actually achieving the measured KPI. The refined FR is atomic and solution-neutral by focusing on the functional obligation—enabling the cycle to execute within the time envelope—without prescribing how (e.g., via speed optimization, path planning, operator training, or equipment design). The actual achievement of p95 ≤ CT_target is a verification criterion, not a functional requirement. This separation allows DPs to address enablement independently of operational performance measurement.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Demonstrate that under nominal duty cycle and site conditions, the NGHE solution's design permits completion of load→travel→dump within CT_target. Measure p95 cycle time across representative operational scenarios; if p95 ≤ CT_target is consistently achieved, the FR is satisfied. If not, root-cause analysis determines whether the failure is in DP (design does not enable it) or in IM/operational factors (design is sound but execution/training/site conditions prevent it).
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-002.1: The NGHE solution shall enable load acquisition within the load phase time budget.
FR-NGHE-002.2: The NGHE solution shall enable travel (loaded and unloaded) within the travel phase time budget.
FR-NGHE-002.3: The NGHE solution shall enable material dump within the dump phase time budget.
FR-NGHE-002.4: The NGHE solution shall minimize idle/transition time between load, travel, and dump phases.

candidate_dps: DP1: Decompose the load→travel→dump cycle into independent sub-processes (load, travel, dump) with dedicated control logic and resource allocation per phase, enabling phase-level optimization and fault isolation.
DP2: Implement a unified state machine that orchestrates all cycle phases through a single control entity, with phase transitions triggered by sensor feedback and timing constraints.
DP3: Allocate fixed time budgets to each phase (load_time, travel_time, dump_time) with hard scheduling constraints, enforcing cycle time compliance through deterministic sequencing.
DP4: Use dynamic phase duration adjustment based on real-time worksite conditions (load density, terrain, distance) with feedback control to meet p95 cycle time target.
DP5: Implement parallel execution of non-blocking sub-tasks (e.g., travel preparation during load phase) to reduce idle time and compress overall cycle duration.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP1 + DP4 (Hybrid): Decompose the load→travel→dump cycle into independent sub-processes with dedicated control logic per phase (DP1 structural foundation), augmented with dynamic phase duration adjustment via feedback control (DP4 adaptive tuning) to meet p95 ≤ CT_target under variable site conditions.

potential_sources: Phase-to-phase handoff timing: Load completion must signal travel readiness; travel completion must signal dump readiness. Loose coupling via event-driven transitions reduces but does not eliminate temporal dependencies.
Resource contention: If load and travel phases share actuators or power budgets, phase independence is compromised. Requires explicit resource reservation protocol.
Feedback loop latency: Real-time condition sensing and phase adjustment (DP4) introduces control loop delay; if sensor-to-actuation latency exceeds phase duration tolerance, p95 target may be missed.
Worksite variability: DP4 assumes predictable feedback signals (load mass, terrain resistance, distance). Extreme outliers or sensor noise can degrade p95 compliance.
coupling_check.notes: DP1 provides structural independence (each phase has its own control logic and can be optimized separately). DP4 introduces a feedback loop that couples phase durations to observed conditions, creating a control dependency. This is acceptable if feedback latency is < 10% of phase duration and sensor reliability is ≥ 99%. Recommend explicit decoupling via phase-level state machines with bounded transition windows.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Cycle time target CT_target is achievable given the physical constraints of the load, travel distance, and dump mechanism under nominal site conditions. RISK: If CT_target is set below the physical minimum (e.g., load mass requires > 30s to load), no DP can satisfy it. Mitigation: Validate CT_target against baseline time-motion study.

ASSUMPTION: Worksite conditions (load density, terrain, distance) vary within a bounded range and are measurable via available sensors. RISK: Extreme outliers (e.g., boulder in load, impassable terrain) will exceed p95 target. Mitigation: Define outlier handling policy (e.g., manual intervention, cycle abort, extended timeout).

ASSUMPTION: Phase transitions are triggered by completion signals (load full, travel arrived, dump empty) rather than fixed timers. RISK: Sensor failures or false positives delay transitions and inflate cycle time. Mitigation: Implement watchdog timers and sensor redundancy.

ASSUMPTION: Feedback control loop (DP4) can adjust phase durations without violating safety constraints (e.g., speed limits, load stability). RISK: Aggressive phase compression may trigger safety violations. Mitigation: Enforce hard constraints on phase duration bounds and acceleration profiles.

ASSUMPTION: p95 metric is measured under the agreed duty cycle (e.g., 8-hour shift, specific load distribution). RISK: Measurement bias if duty cycle does not reflect worst-case conditions. Mitigation: Validate p95 across multiple duty cycles and site conditions.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR (refined): The NGHE solution shall enable execution of the load→travel→dump cycle with measured p95 cycle time ≤ CT_target under the agreed duty cycle and site conditions, subject to hard constraints on load stability, speed limits, and operator safety policies. DP (refined): Decompose the cycle into three independent sub-processes (load, travel, dump), each with dedicated control logic and resource allocation. Implement phase transitions via event-driven state machine (completion signals with watchdog timeouts). Augment with adaptive feedback control that adjusts phase durations based on real-time worksite conditions (load mass, terrain resistance, distance), subject to hard bounds on phase duration and acceleration. Validate p95 compliance through simulation and field testing across multiple duty cycles and site conditions. Define outlier handling policy (manual intervention, cycle abort, extended timeout) for conditions exceeding feedback control authority.

im_boundary.name: Vehicle
im_boundary.scope: Autonomous cycle control logic (load→travel→dump phases), phase transition state machine, dynamic duration adjustment via onboard feedback sensors, and real-time cycle performance monitoring.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle reports cycle phase state, duration metrics, p95 cycle time, and sensor feedback (load mass, position, terrain resistance) to enable external monitoring and adaptive tuning validation.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle receives cycle initiation commands and CT_target setpoint updates from NGHE Solution; tasking also conveys site condition context (terrain type, expected load range) to inform phase duration baseline.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle receives safety constraints (e.g., max speed, load limits) and reports safety-critical faults (sensor failures, phase timeout violations) to Safety Services for intervention.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle maintains low-latency communication with Teleoperation and NGHE Solution for remote monitoring, emergency override, and dynamic parameter updates during operation.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent containment: Vehicle owns the physical actuators, onboard sensors (load cell, position, terrain feedback), and real-time control loop. DP1 (phase decomposition + state machine) and DP4 (feedback-driven duration adjustment) are both naturally localized to vehicle firmware/embedded controller. Minor diffusion: CT_target setpoint and site condition context arrive via Tasking (external), and telemetry export is necessary for NGHE Solution validation.

Poor containment: NGHE Solution is a supervisory/orchestration layer, not a real-time control executor. Placing DP1+DP4 here would require Vehicle to be a passive actuator proxy, introducing high latency and diffusion of phase logic across network boundary. Feedback control (DP4) demands sub-100ms loop closure; network round-trip latency makes this infeasible. Not recommended.

Very poor containment: Teleoperation is a human-in-the-loop interface, not an autonomous control module. DP1+DP4 require deterministic, real-time execution independent of operator input. Placing cycle logic here would create tight coupling to network latency and operator availability, violating autonomous operation requirement. Major diffusion across Vehicle and Safety Services.

Poor containment: Safety Services is a constraint-enforcement and monitoring layer. While it validates phase transitions and enforces safety limits (via SafetyAlert), it should not own the core cycle control logic. DP1+DP4 are functional/performance concerns, not safety concerns. Placing them here conflates safety monitoring with operational control, reducing modularity and increasing coupling to Vehicle.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Vehicle module is the natural owner of DP1+DP4 because it encapsulates the physical plant (actuators, sensors, inertia) and the real-time control loop required for phase decomposition and feedback-driven duration adjustment. The hybrid DP (structural + adaptive) maps cleanly to vehicle firmware: DP1 (phase state machine and sub-process logic) is the control architecture, and DP4 (dynamic tuning via onboard sensor feedback) is the adaptive layer. External interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link) provide necessary coordination and monitoring without diffusing core cycle logic.
containment_risks: Risk: Onboard sensor noise or failure (e.g., load cell drift, position sensor glitch) could corrupt DP4 feedback loop, causing phase duration oscillation or timeout. Mitigation: Implement sensor validation and fallback to nominal phase durations if feedback quality degrades.

Risk: Network latency in Tasking (CT_target updates) or SafetyAlert (constraint changes) could cause Vehicle to operate with stale parameters. Mitigation: Vehicle caches last-known-good setpoints and constraints; updates are applied only at phase boundaries to avoid mid-phase disruption.

Risk: DP4 adaptive tuning may over-fit to local site conditions, reducing generalization to new sites. Mitigation: NGHE Solution monitors Telemetry and adjusts CT_target setpoint or provides site-specific baseline phase durations via Tasking to guide Vehicle tuning.

Risk: Tight coupling between DP1 (phase logic) and DP4 (feedback control) in Vehicle firmware could make testing and validation difficult. Mitigation: Implement DP1 as a deterministic state machine with DP4 as a pluggable feedback module; allow DP1 to operate in open-loop (nominal durations) for validation.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: No revision to DP choice is necessary. DP1+DP4 (hybrid structural + adaptive) is well-suited to Vehicle containment. However, clarify the feedback control loop: specify which onboard sensors (load cell, position, terrain resistance proxy) drive DP4 tuning, and define the adaptation algorithm (e.g., proportional-integral control, lookup table, machine learning model). If onboard sensing is insufficient to close the loop reliably, consider a two-tier approach: Vehicle executes DP1 (phase decomposition) with nominal durations, and NGHE Solution (via Tasking) provides DP4 tuning recommendations based on aggregated Telemetry data from multiple cycles. This preserves Vehicle autonomy while leveraging centralized learning.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP1+DP4 (hybrid decomposition + adaptive feedback control) directly addresses the FR requirement to execute load→travel→dump within CT_target. DP1 provides structural phase independence and deterministic sequencing; DP4 adds dynamic duration adjustment to handle variable site conditions. The combination is logically sound and maps to the acceptance criterion p95 ≤ CT_target. No logical gaps or contradictions detected.

diffusion_risks: Minor diffusion: CT_target setpoint and site condition context arrive via external Tasking interface. Mitigation: Vehicle caches parameters and applies updates only at phase boundaries to preserve real-time loop integrity.

Minor diffusion: DP4 feedback control depends on onboard sensor quality (load cell, position, terrain resistance proxy). Risk of sensor noise/failure corrupting adaptation loop. Mitigation: Implement sensor validation and fallback to nominal durations if feedback degrades below quality threshold.

Moderate diffusion risk if DP4 tuning algorithm is not clearly specified: unclear whether adaptation is purely onboard (proportional-integral, lookup table) or hybrid (Vehicle executes DP1 nominally, NGHE Solution provides DP4 recommendations via Tasking based on aggregated Telemetry). Recommend explicit algorithm specification to avoid ambiguity.

Coupling risk: DP1 (phase state machine) and DP4 (feedback module) tightly integrated in Vehicle firmware. Risk: difficult to test DP1 in isolation or validate phase logic independently of adaptive tuning. Mitigation: Implement DP1 as deterministic state machine with DP4 as pluggable feedback layer; allow open-loop operation for validation.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Vehicle module achieves 0.92 containment score with 0.08 diffusion. Excellent localization: Vehicle owns physical actuators, onboard sensors, real-time control loop, and phase state machine. DP1 (structural decomposition) and DP4 (adaptive tuning) both map naturally to vehicle firmware/embedded controller. External interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link) provide necessary coordination without diffusing core cycle logic. Alternative candidates (NGHE Solution, Teleoperation, Safety Services) all score poorly (0.3–0.45 containment) due to latency, human-in-the-loop coupling, or safety-vs-operational conflation. Vehicle is the correct owner.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. Acceptance criterion p95 ≤ CT_target is measurable via Vehicle Telemetry (cycle phase state, duration metrics, p95 aggregation). Vehicle reports cycle performance metrics continuously; NGHE Solution can validate p95 against CT_target setpoint. Sensor feedback (load mass, position, terrain resistance) is also exported via Telemetry, enabling external validation of DP4 adaptation logic. Worksite safety plan compliance is enforced via SafetyAlert interface (Vehicle reports safety-critical faults; Safety Services intervenes). Verification is feasible with standard instrumentation and monitoring.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. Vehicle is unambiguously allocated ownership of DP1+DP4 execution. Tasking interface specifies CT_target setpoint and site condition context (inputs to DP4 baseline tuning). Telemetry interface exports cycle metrics and sensor feedback for NGHE Solution validation. SafetyAlert enforces safety constraints without diffusing cycle logic. Network Link provides low-latency coordination for remote monitoring and emergency override. Allocation boundaries are well-defined; no ambiguity about which module executes which DP component.

recommended_revisions: Clarify DP4 feedback control algorithm: specify whether adaptation is purely onboard (e.g., proportional-integral control, lookup table indexed by load/terrain) or hybrid (Vehicle executes DP1 nominally, NGHE Solution provides DP4 tuning recommendations via Tasking based on aggregated Telemetry). If onboard sensing is insufficient for reliable closed-loop control, adopt two-tier approach: Vehicle owns DP1 (deterministic phase decomposition with nominal durations), NGHE Solution owns DP4 (centralized learning and tuning recommendations).

Define sensor validation and fallback strategy for DP4: specify quality thresholds for load cell, position sensor, and terrain resistance proxy; if feedback quality degrades below threshold, Vehicle reverts to nominal phase durations and reports sensor fault via Telemetry and SafetyAlert.

Implement DP1 as pluggable state machine: separate phase logic (DP1) from feedback adaptation (DP4) in firmware architecture to enable independent testing and validation. Allow Vehicle to operate in open-loop mode (DP1 only, nominal durations) for baseline cycle time validation before enabling DP4 adaptive tuning.

Specify parameter update protocol for Tasking interface: define how CT_target setpoint and site condition context are delivered (e.g., at cycle initiation, at phase boundaries, or continuously). Ensure Vehicle caches last-known-good parameters and applies updates only at safe points (phase boundaries) to avoid mid-phase disruption.

Add telemetry export specification: define which metrics are reported (cycle phase state, phase duration, p95 cycle time, load mass, position, terrain resistance, sensor quality flags, adaptation parameters) and reporting frequency (per-cycle, per-phase, or continuous). Enable NGHE Solution to validate DP4 effectiveness and adjust CT_target or site-specific baselines accordingly.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass logically and architecturally, confirming that DP1+DP4 is sound and Vehicle is the correct owner. However, the recommended_revisions identify critical ambiguities and coupling risks that must be resolved before proceeding to sub-level decomposition. Specifically: (1) DP4 feedback control algorithm is underspecified (onboard vs. hybrid vs. centralized learning), creating uncertainty about feasibility and testability; (2) DP1 and DP4 are tightly coupled in firmware, blocking independent validation of phase logic; (3) sensor validation and fallback strategy lack explicit definition, risking safety and reliability. Given high safety criticality, these ambiguities must be clarified at the current level before decomposing further. Proceeding to sub-FRs without resolving DP4 specification and DP1-DP4 decoupling would propagate uncertainty into lower levels and complicate verification. A targeted revision cycle (clarify DP4 algorithm choice, decouple DP1 state machine from DP4 feedback layer, define sensor validation thresholds) will harden the design and enable confident sub-level decomposition.

revise.why: DP4 (adaptive feedback control) requires explicit algorithm specification to resolve ambiguity between onboard closed-loop control, hybrid (Vehicle + NGHE Solution), and centralized learning. Current DP4 statement is too abstract and creates coupling risk with DP1. Recommend: (A) If onboard sensing is reliable (load cell, position, terrain resistance proxy all validated), specify DP4 as onboard proportional-integral or lookup-table adaptation with explicit tuning parameters and sensor quality thresholds. (B) If onboard sensing is insufficient, specify DP4 as two-tier: Vehicle executes DP1 (deterministic phase decomposition with nominal durations), NGHE Solution owns DP4 (centralized learning from aggregated Telemetry, provides tuning recommendations via Tasking). (C) Decouple DP1 (phase state machine, deterministic sequencing) from DP4 (feedback adaptation) in firmware architecture: DP1 operates as pluggable state machine with nominal phase durations; DP4 is optional feedback layer that modulates durations if enabled. This decoupling enables independent testing of DP1 (open-loop validation) and DP4 (closed-loop validation). Revised DP statement should specify algorithm choice, sensor validation thresholds, fallback strategy, and firmware decoupling approach.

assumptions: Vehicle has onboard sensors (load cell, position sensor, terrain resistance proxy) with sufficient fidelity to support closed-loop feedback control. If this assumption is invalid, DP4 must shift to hybrid or centralized architecture.

Tasking interface can deliver CT_target setpoint and site condition context with sufficient latency (≤ phase boundary interval) to enable safe parameter updates. If latency exceeds phase duration, parameter caching and update-at-boundary strategy is mandatory.

SafetyAlert interface provides reliable, low-latency safety constraint enforcement without blocking Vehicle cycle execution. If safety intervention requires stopping the Vehicle mid-cycle, DP1 phase logic must include safe stop states.

Network Link provides sufficient bandwidth and reliability for continuous Telemetry export (cycle metrics, sensor feedback, adaptation parameters) without impacting Vehicle real-time control loop. If network is unreliable, Telemetry buffering and local logging are required.

NGHE Solution can aggregate Telemetry from multiple Vehicles and compute DP4 tuning recommendations (or validate onboard adaptation) with sufficient frequency to enable site-specific baseline updates. If aggregation latency is high, onboard adaptation must be self-contained.
open_risks: DP4 algorithm ambiguity: unclear whether adaptation is onboard (proportional-integral, lookup table), hybrid (Vehicle + NGHE Solution), or centralized (NGHE Solution only). This ambiguity blocks firmware design, testing strategy, and verification plan. Mitigation: explicit algorithm choice and specification before sub-level decomposition.

DP1-DP4 coupling: phase state machine and feedback adaptation are tightly integrated in Vehicle firmware, blocking independent validation of phase logic. Risk: if DP4 feedback is noisy or miscalibrated, it corrupts DP1 phase sequencing, making it impossible to isolate root cause. Mitigation: decouple DP1 and DP4 in firmware architecture; allow open-loop operation (DP1 only) for baseline validation.

Sensor validation and fallback: DP4 depends on onboard sensor quality (load cell, position, terrain resistance proxy), but quality thresholds and fallback strategy are not explicitly defined. Risk: sensor noise, drift, or failure corrupts adaptation loop, causing cycle time degradation or safety violations. Mitigation: define sensor quality thresholds, implement validation logic, specify fallback to nominal durations if feedback degrades.

Parameter update protocol: Tasking interface delivers CT_target setpoint and site condition context, but update frequency and timing are not specified. Risk: mid-phase parameter updates disrupt phase logic or cause inconsistent state. Mitigation: define parameter caching strategy and update-at-boundary protocol.

Telemetry export specification: metrics and reporting frequency are not explicitly defined. Risk: NGHE Solution cannot validate DP4 effectiveness or adjust site-specific baselines without clear visibility into Vehicle adaptation behavior. Mitigation: specify telemetry schema (phase state, duration, p95, load, position, terrain resistance, sensor quality, adaptation parameters) and reporting frequency.
evidence_needed: Onboard sensor capability assessment: validate that load cell, position sensor, and terrain resistance proxy (if available) have sufficient fidelity, latency, and reliability to support closed-loop feedback control. If assessment shows insufficient fidelity, recommend hybrid or centralized DP4 architecture.

DP4 algorithm specification: explicit choice between onboard, hybrid, or centralized adaptation, with detailed algorithm description (proportional-integral gains, lookup table structure, learning rate, tuning frequency, sensor quality thresholds, fallback logic).

Firmware architecture design: decouple DP1 (phase state machine) from DP4 (feedback adaptation) to enable independent testing. Specify how DP4 is plugged into DP1 (e.g., duration modulation at phase boundaries, optional feedback layer).

Sensor validation and fallback specification: define quality thresholds for each sensor (load cell accuracy, position sensor drift, terrain resistance proxy confidence), implement validation logic, specify fallback to nominal durations if feedback degrades.

Parameter update protocol specification: define how CT_target setpoint and site condition context are delivered via Tasking (at cycle initiation, at phase boundaries, or continuously), how Vehicle caches and applies updates, and how mid-phase disruption is prevented.

Telemetry export specification: define schema (cycle phase state, phase duration, p95 cycle time, load mass, position, terrain resistance, sensor quality flags, adaptation parameters) and reporting frequency (per-cycle, per-phase, or continuous).

Verification and validation plan: specify how DP1 is validated in open-loop mode (deterministic phase sequencing, nominal cycle time), how DP4 is validated in closed-loop mode (adaptation effectiveness, sensor quality impact), and how p95 ≤ CT_target is measured and reported.
